common_name: White Marseille Fig (White Fig of Marseille)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; White Marseille is one of the oldest known fig cultivars in continuous cultivation, grown in European gardens for centuries and valued for its exceptionally sweet, honey-vanilla flavor and pale greenish-yellow skin ripening to creamy white.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; figs tolerate poor, dry soils better than most fruit trees and perform well in raised beds and large containers where root pruning encourages fruiting. White Marseille (also spelled 'Marseilles') is one of the oldest fig varieties in documented cultivation, known in European gardens since at least the 16th century and praised in early horticultural literature for its sweet, honey-like flavor; the medium to large, turbinate fruits have pale greenish-yellow to creamy-white skin and translucent white to pale amber interior flesh with a mild, delicate sweetness and gentle fig fragrance unlike the darker, richer intensity of dark figs. A common fig type that requires no pollinator; produces a reliable main crop (August–September) and often a smaller breba crop in early summer. Excellent container and espalier candidate; protect from frost below 15°F as a large container specimen that can be moved indoors.
sources: University of California Riverside Citrus Variety Collection

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Conadria was selected by USDA breeder Ira Condit in 1955 for outstanding fresh-eating and drying quality, large attractive fruit, and high productivity — a premium commercial and home-garden fig variety particularly suited to hot, dry California and Western conditions.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile to average soil in full sun; Conadria is a common fig type (no pollinator needed) and one of the most productive figs per tree, bearing heavily in its second and third years. Conadria was released in 1955 by USDA fig breeder Ira J. Condit from the Riverside California station after decades of fig selection work; it was selected for exceptional fruit quality combined with high yields, producing large (3–5 oz), turbinate, greenish-white to pale yellow fruits with strawberry-pink interior flesh and very sweet, rich flavor comparable to the finest White Adriatic and Calimyrna types. Outstanding for fresh eating and particularly excellent for drying, producing firm, sweet dried figs with good shelf life. A vigorous, upright, productive tree that performs exceptionally in hot, dry Western climates (Central Valley California, Arizona, Texas, Gulf Coast) and ripens reliably in the main crop (August–October). Somewhat more heat-tolerant than many high-quality white figs.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container trees in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Desert King is the most widely grown and most reliably productive fig for the Pacific Northwest and similar cool-summer climates, where it produces an abundant breba crop that ripens reliably even in cool, short summers that defeat most other fig varieties.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile to average soil in full sun against a south-facing wall in cooler climates to maximize heat accumulation; Desert King is a San Pedro type that requires no pollinator for its reliable breba crop but benefits from caprification for main crop development in warm climates. Desert King is the dominant fig cultivar in Pacific Northwest gardens (Oregon, Washington, British Columbia) where it excels for its large, spectacular breba crop (early summer, June–July) that ripens reliably without the extended heat required by most other figs; the very large (3–6 oz), flattened-turbinate fruits have thick, green to greenish-yellow ribbed skin and dark strawberry to amber-red interior flesh with rich, sweet flavor. In warm climates (California, Gulf Coast) it also produces a substantial main crop in August–October. Among the most cold-hardy of all quality fig varieties, surviving to about 10–12°F when established and dormant. A vigorous, large tree that requires space; well-suited to training as an espalier against south-facing masonry walls.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, average to moderately fertile soil in full sun; Brunswick has been grown in the southeastern United States for generations under the name 'Magnolia fig' and is one of the most widely distributed Southern heirloom figs, well-adapted to humid Gulf Coast conditions and reliable in zones 7–10.

directions: Plant in well-drained, average soil in full sun; Brunswick is a common fig type requiring no pollinator and one of the most widely grown figs in the American South under various regional names. Brunswick (known throughout the southeastern United States as 'Magnolia fig' and occasionally as 'Madonna fig') is an old European fig variety that has been grown in Southern gardens for at least 150 years, widely distributed through cuttings exchanged between generations of gardeners in the Gulf Coast states; the very large (3–5 oz), pear-shaped to oblong fruits have thin, pale yellowish-green to bronze-green skin, prominent ribs, and dark amber to brownish-strawberry interior flesh with moderately sweet flavor and good quality. A vigorous, large-growing tree that tolerates the heat and humidity of Gulf Coast summers better than many European fig varieties. Excellent for preserves, fresh eating, and drying. Because of widespread cutting-sharing over generations, plants sold or shared under the name 'Magnolia fig' may vary somewhat; most are similar to or identical with Brunswick.

common_name: Col de Dame Blanc Fig (Neck of the Lady Fig)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun against a warm south-facing wall for best fruit development; Col de Dame Blanc is a rare and highly sought-after Spanish heirloom from the Catalonia region particularly associated with Sitges, prized by serious fig collectors and chefs for an extraordinary, concentrated honey sweetness that many consider unmatched among all fig varieties.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile to average soil in full sun; Col de Dame Blanc performs best with reflected heat from south or west-facing walls and requires genuine summer heat to ripen its fruit fully — not recommended for cool-summer Pacific Northwest climates. Col de Dame (literally 'neck of the lady' in Catalan, for the fruit's distinctive elongated, graceful neck) is a group of heirloom Spanish figs from the Catalonia region particularly associated with the town of Sitges; the Blanc (white) form produces elongated, pear-necked, greenish-white to pale yellow fruits with exceptionally thin skin and almost liqueur-like white interior flesh with a concentrated honey sweetness and complex figgy aroma that experienced fig growers frequently describe as among the finest flavors of any variety. A common fig type requiring no pollinator. Less cold-hardy and less vigorous than many commercial varieties but the extraordinary flavor rewards the extra attention; increasingly popular among American fig collectors and now more readily available from specialty nurseries than a decade ago.
sources: Spanish Ministry of Agriculture Heirloom Variety Registry
